title: Ameriprise Financial, Inc. (AMP) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Ameriprise Financial (AMP) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1. Economic Prospect Score: 74/100 — Moderate Prospect.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A 6.5% growth rate reflects the powerful secular tailwinds of wealth transfer and retirement planning for the mass-affluent. AMP's shift toward recurring, fee-based advisory revenue provides a stable foundation for compounding, though growth is partially dependent on broader equity market appreciation.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.0% discount rate is utilized. While AMP is an extremely high-quality financial firm, its revenue is inherently tethered to the volatility of global equity and bond markets. This market sensitivity requires a higher risk premium than a pure consumer staple or utility.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A conservative 2.0% terminal growth rate assumes AMP will grow slightly slower than broader U.S. GDP in perpetuity, acknowledging the mature nature of the domestic wealth management industry and persistent fee compression pressures.

thesisParagraphs: Ameriprise Financial is frequently misclassified by casual observers as a legacy insurance company. In reality, it has executed a masterful, multi-year pivot into a premier, capital-light wealth and asset manager. Over 80% of its revenue is now generated from its Advice & Wealth Management segment, a business characterized by sticky client relationships, recurring fees, and high returns on invested capital.

Despite this transformation, the market often values AMP at a discount to pure-play wealth management peers or mega-banks. Its massive free cash flow generation is consistently deployed toward aggressive share repurchases and dividend growth. Trading at roughly 12x forward earnings, AMP presents a compelling margin of safety. It is a highly efficient compounding machine operating in a demographic sweet spot—the aging mass-affluent population seeking comprehensive financial planning.
verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: Why did Gemini pick a 6.5% growth rate for AMP?
faq.answer: This growth rate models the company's consistent ability to gather new client assets and transition them into fee-based advisory accounts. It assumes moderate organic growth combined with the historical tailwind of long-term equity market appreciation.

faq.question: Is AMP vulnerable to a stock market crash?
faq.answer: Yes. A significant portion of AMP's revenue is derived from fees assessed on total Assets Under Management (AUM). A severe market downturn would mechanically reduce AUM and, consequently, its fee revenue, though its comprehensive planning approach helps mitigate client panic-selling.

faq.question: Why is AMP considered undervalued?
faq.answer: The model suggests AMP is undervalued because the market continues to price it somewhat like a traditional, capital-heavy financial institution rather than the capital-light, high-return wealth management franchise it has become. Its aggressive share buyback program further amplifies intrinsic value per share.

prospectVerdictDetail: Ameriprise Financial is a powerful wealth management franchise, increasingly driven by its sticky, high-margin advisory business. While it operates in a highly competitive and fragmented market against massive banking institutions, its targeted approach toward mass-affluent clients provides a durable niche. The transition away from capital-intensive annuities toward fee-based asset management has fundamentally improved its return on equity. Strong capital return policies and consistent asset gathering underscore its structural advantages.

competitiveMomentum.title: Competitive Momentum
competitiveMomentum.summary: AMP's competitive momentum is driven by its large, dedicated advisor force and the ongoing structural shift toward fee-based wealth management, allowing it to organically compound client assets.

factors.name: Revenue Growth vs. Peers
factors.rationale: AMP delivers solid mid-single-digit organic growth, frequently bolstered by market appreciation of assets under management (AUM). It performs well within its mass-affluent niche but lacks the explosive growth of purely digital-native fintech disruptors.

factors.name: Market Share Trajectory
factors.rationale: While the wealth management space is consolidating, AMP has successfully defended and slightly grown its share by aggressively recruiting experienced advisors and acquiring complementary asset management capabilities.

factors.name: Pricing Power
factors.rationale: Advisory fees are generally sticky, but the broader industry faces persistent fee compression from low-cost passive index funds and robo-advisors. AMP counters this by emphasizing comprehensive financial planning rather than just stock picking.

factors.name: Product Velocity
factors.rationale: AMP continuously refines its digital platforms and broadens its alternative investment offerings to remain competitive. The integration of AI tools for advisors is a key focus, though adoption is inherently slower in relationship-driven wealth management.

moatDurability.title: Moat Durability
moatDurability.summary: The primary moat for Ameriprise is the high switching costs associated with moving financial advisors, coupled with the trust built through comprehensive, long-term financial planning relationships.

factors.name: Switching Costs
factors.rationale: Moving entire wealth portfolios, retirement accounts, and complex estate plans is highly frictional for clients. This creates significant stickiness, particularly in AMP's core demographic of pre-retirees who value the personal advisor relationship.

factors.name: Network Effects
factors.rationale: Network effects are minimal in traditional wealth management. However, its scale does provide some advantage in attracting top-tier advisors who seek a robust technology platform and compliance infrastructure.

factors.name: Regulatory & IP Position
factors.rationale: AMP operates in a heavily regulated environment (SEC, FINRA). While it has no significant intellectual property moat, its massive compliance apparatus serves as a barrier to entry for smaller, independent firms.

factors.name: Capital Intensity Advantage
factors.rationale: As AMP has systematically de-risked and spun off its capital-intensive legacy insurance lines, it has transitioned into a highly capital-light, fee-generating business, dramatically improving its return on invested capital.

sentimentCatalyst.title: Sentiment & Catalysts
sentimentCatalyst.summary: Sentiment is bolstered by AMP's exceptional record of capital returns to shareholders and its successful pivot to a higher-quality earnings mix, though it remains sensitive to broader macroeconomic and equity market cycles.

factors.name: Earnings Estimate Revisions
factors.rationale: Earnings estimates have remained robust, supported by strong equity markets driving AUM fees higher and higher interest rates temporarily boosting the yield on client cash sweeps.

factors.name: News & Narrative Sentiment
factors.rationale: The narrative is highly positive, focusing on the firm's successful transformation into a premier wealth manager. Recognition like Forbes Top Women Wealth Advisors reinforces its strong brand reputation in a crowded market.

factors.name: Management & Capital Allocation
factors.rationale: Management's capital allocation has been exemplary. The firm aggressively repurchases shares and consistently raises its dividend, returning the vast majority of its free cash flow to shareholders while making disciplined, bolt-on acquisitions.

prospectKeyRisks: A sustained equity or fixed-income bear market would directly compress assets under management (AUM), leading to a proportional decline in fee-based revenue.
Intensifying competition from mega-banks (like Morgan Stanley and Bank of America) and low-cost digital brokerages could accelerate fee compression and advisor attrition.
Regulatory changes, particularly regarding fiduciary standards or the monetization of client cash sweeps, could negatively impact high-margin revenue streams.
prospectKeyCatalysts: The $84 trillion intergenerational wealth transfer over the next two decades disproportionately benefits advice-centric firms as inheritors seek professional guidance for complex estate and tax planning needs
Continued share repurchase program reducing float by 3-5% annually provides mechanical EPS growth that compounds meaningfully over multi-year holding periods
Expansion of banking and lending products within the advisor channel could deepen client wallet share and improve revenue per advisor without proportional cost increases
prospectMethodology: Consensus Analysis — Economic Prospect Score averaging independent evaluations from Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1. Gemini scored AMP at 76/100 and Opus at 74/100. Each factor score is the arithmetic mean of both models. Three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30).
